name: Prince Edward Island
history: Prince Edward Island was originally inhabited by the Mi'kmaq people for thousands of years before European contact. French settlers established Acadia on the island in the early 1600s, but the British took control in 1758 and expelled many Acadians during the Seven Years' War. The island became a separate British colony in 1769 and joined Canadian Confederation in 1873, playing a crucial role in the Dominion's formation. Today, PEI celebrates this layered heritage through its museums, historical sites, and cultural traditions.
culturalNotes: Prince Edward Island is renowned for its literary heritage, particularly as the birthplace of Lucy Maud Montgomery's Anne of Green Gables, which continues to shape the island's identity and tourism. The island maintains strong Acadian and Scottish traditions, reflected in its music, festivals, and food culture, with seafood and potato-based dishes featuring prominently in local cuisine. PEI's tight-knit communities, vibrant arts scene, and commitment to preserving its rural character make it a distinctly charming and culturally rich destination.
bestTimeToVisit: Visit in summer (June-September) for warm ocean temperatures and beach activities.
languageNotes: English is the primary language spoken on Prince Edward Island, though French speakers are present, especially in Acadian communities. Visitors may encounter a gentle Maritime accent and local expressions like "hey" as a conversational filler, and the island's friendliness means directions and recommendations are readily offered by residents.

places.name: Charlottetown
places.description: The capital and largest city of PEI, known as the birthplace of Canadian Confederation. It features Victorian architecture, the famous Confederation Centre of the Arts, and serves as the cultural and commercial hub of the island.

activities.detail: This iconic cultural venue hosts theatrical productions, including the famous Anne of Green Gables musical, along with art galleries and a library.
A scenic waterfront park offering walking trails, picnic areas, and views of Charlottetown Harbour with access to the War Memorial.
The official residence of the Lieutenant Governor features beautifully preserved Victorian architecture and guided tours of its elegant rooms.
A vibrant waterfront development with shops, restaurants, and galleries housed in restored historic buildings along the harbor.
An impressive Gothic Revival cathedral with ornate interior details and stained glass windows that dominates the city skyline.
A pleasant walking path along the harbor offering public art installations, benches, and panoramic views of the water and city.

food.detail: Fresh, plump mussels from local waters served steamed in white wine, garlic, and herbs, a signature Maritime dish.
Succulent PEI lobster meat served chilled on a toasted bun with minimal mayo, showcasing the premium local catch.
Briny oysters harvested from Charlottetown waters, best enjoyed raw on the half-shell at waterfront restaurants.
A traditional Acadian comfort dish made with grated potatoes, chicken or seafood, and broth, baked until golden brown.
Light sponge cake layered with whipped cream and fresh PEI strawberries, a seasonal specialty celebrating local fruit.
Battered and fried Atlantic fish served with crispy chips and tartar sauce, a classic Maritime tradition found throughout the city.

culture.detail: Charlottetown hosts numerous festivals from June to September, including the Charlottetown Festival and Canada Day celebrations, so plan accordingly.
The city takes pride in its role as the birthplace of Canadian Confederation in 1867; historical plaques and sites throughout downtown commemorate this significance.
The literary character Anne of Green Gables holds special cultural significance here; locals embrace the heritage and many businesses reference the beloved character.
Residents are known for their warmth and unhurried approach to life; it's customary to greet shopkeepers and strike up conversations with locals.

places.name: Cavendish Beach
places.description: One of Canada's most famous beaches with distinctive red sand, clear waters, and a family-friendly atmosphere. It's the gateway to Prince Edward Island National Park and inspired the Anne of Green Gables novels.
imageQueries: Prince Edward Island National Park beach

activities.detail: Wade into the warm, shallow waters or relax on the distinctive red sand beach that stretches for miles along the north shore.
Visit the home that inspired Lucy Maud Montgomery's beloved novels and explore period furnishings and literary memorabilia.
Walk the scenic coastal path through Prince Edward Island National Park with views of red cliffs, dunes, and forest ecosystems.
Enjoy family rides, mini golf, and arcade games on the strip just behind the beach for classic seaside entertainment.

activities.text: Beachcombing for Sea Glass and Shells
activities.detail: Search the shoreline for smoothed sea glass, colorful shells, and driftwood polished by the tides.
Capture the golden hour over the red sand and calm waters, a photographer's favorite spot on PEI's north shore.

food.detail: Sample locally harvested mussels steamed in white wine or cream at beachside restaurants, a signature Maritime delicacy.
Try the iconic PEI lobster roll with fresh Atlantic lobster meat served on a toasted bun at casual beach vendors or nearby eateries.
Order battered Atlantic cod or haddock with crispy fries from a local chip stand, perfect for eating while watching the sunset.
Indulge in fresh strawberry pie, shortcake, or jam made from the island's famous berries at local bakeries and ice cream shops.

food.text: Anne of Green Gables Tea Experience
food.detail: Enjoy traditional afternoon tea with scones and local treats inspired by the novels at the Anne of Green Gables Museum tearoom.

culture.detail: The distinctive red sand is delicate and irreplaceable; stay on designated paths in the national park to prevent erosion.
The local community deeply cherishes Lucy Maud Montgomery's literary legacy, and you'll find references throughout shops, museums, and attractions.
July and August are the busiest months with warm waters and packed beaches; visit in June or September for fewer crowds and pleasant weather.
Cavendish Beach is part of Prince Edward Island National Park, so a park pass is required and pets are restricted in certain areas during peak season.

places.name: Green Gables Heritage Place
places.description: The restored house and grounds that inspired Lucy Maud Montgomery's beloved Anne of Green Gables novels, drawing literary pilgrims from around the world. The site offers period-authentic rooms and beautiful gardens overlooking farmland.
imageQueries: Green Gables house Anne of Green Gables

activities.detail: Walk through the period-authentic rooms of the main house decorated with Victorian furnishings and memorabilia that inspired Montgomery's novels.
Explore the beautiful cultivated gardens including flower beds, heritage plants, and quiet pathways that reflect the aesthetic of Montgomery's era.

activities.text: Visit the Anne's Room of Her Own exhibition
activities.detail: Experience an interactive display celebrating Lucy Maud Montgomery's life, writing process, and the cultural impact of Anne of Green Gables.
Follow the woodland path that inspired the fictional Haunted Wood in the novels, featuring interpretive signs and natural forest scenery.

activities.text: Attend a literary program or theatrical performance
activities.detail: Catch seasonal readings, dramatizations, or author talks that bring the Anne stories to life with period-appropriate performances.
Enjoy a meal in designated areas overlooking the farmland and gardens while soaking in the rural Prince Edward Island landscape.

food.detail: A local favorite made with fresh Prince Edward Island strawberries, whipped cream, and homemade or locally-sourced sponge cake, often available at on-site cafés.

food.text: Anne of Green Gables themed afternoon tea
food.detail: A literary-inspired tea service featuring finger sandwiches, scones, and pastries served in period style at the heritage site's tea room.
Fresh local mussels steamed or prepared in butter and white wine, showcasing the island's renowned seafood traditions.
Heritage recipes for strawberry, raspberry, and berry preserves available for purchase, reflecting the domestic traditions depicted in the novels.

culture.detail: The site draws literary tourists worldwide, especially in summer months, so visiting before 11 AM ensures a more peaceful and authentic experience.
The grounds are extensive and involve both indoor house tours and outdoor garden exploration, so wear appropriate footwear and clothing for changing weather.
This is a pilgrimage site for Anne of Green Gables fans worldwide, so maintain a respectful and contemplative attitude while exploring the spaces that inspired the beloved novels.

places.name: Summerside
places.description: PEI's second-largest city with a charming waterfront and bustling cultural scene, home to the International Shellfish Festival. The downtown features restored heritage buildings and excellent restaurants celebrating local seafood.

activities.detail: Stroll the scenic harborfront promenade lined with public art, benches, and views of Bedeque Bay, ideal for sunset walks and spotting local wildlife.
Visit during late summer (typically August) to experience PEI's premier culinary event featuring oysters, mussels, scallops, and live music.
Explore visual arts exhibitions, performances, and cultural programs in this renovated heritage building showcasing regional and national artists.
Walk through beautifully restored Victorian and Edwardian architecture housing boutiques, galleries, and local eateries along Water Street and adjacent blocks.
Discover artisan shops, craft studios, and waterfront dining at this redeveloped historic village featuring local makers and craftspeople.
Experience Scottish bagpipe and Highland dancing performances or lessons at this unique cultural institution celebrating PEI's Celtic heritage.
Catch live theatre productions, concerts, and cultural performances in an intimate venue overlooking the harbor.

food.detail: Fresh-shucked oysters from local waters served raw on the half-shell, a signature delicacy at waterfront restaurants and the Shellfish Festival.
Sweet Prince Edward Island mussels cooked in a traditional garlic and white wine broth, served with crusty bread for soaking up the sauce.
Succulent local lobster meat tossed with light mayo and served on a toasted bun, a classic Maritime preparation found throughout Summerside's dining scene.
Plump local scallops quickly seared and often served with local vegetables or a delicate butter sauce at fine dining establishments.
Traditional soft bread made with local potatoes, a regional staple served warm with meals at heritage restaurants and bakeries.

culture.detail: Plan your visit for late August when Summerside hosts its celebrated International Shellfish Festival, the busiest and most vibrant time of year.
Summerside's compact heritage downtown is best explored on foot, with parking available and most attractions within easy walking distance of each other.
Join locals in watching the sunset from the waterfront boardwalk, a cherished evening ritual that showcases the natural beauty of Bedeque Bay.

places.name: Malpeque Bay
places.description: Famous for its delicious Malpeque oysters and stunning coastal scenery with white sand beaches and red cliffs. The bay offers excellent opportunities for shellfish tours, beach walks, and seafood dining experiences.

activities.detail: Join a guided shellfish harvesting tour to learn how Malpeque oysters are farmed and enjoy fresh oysters directly from the water.
Walk along pristine white sand beaches and explore the red sandstone cliffs that characterize the Malpeque Bay coastline.
Stroll along the shoreline at dusk to witness the dramatic red cliffs glowing in golden light and observe local seabirds.
Browse dockside fish markets and seafood stands to purchase fresh Malpeque oysters, lobster, and mussels directly from harvesters.
Visit designated viewpoints around the bay to photograph the distinctive rust-colored geological formations and coastal landscape.

food.detail: These renowned briny oysters with a sweet finish are best enjoyed raw on the half-shell or grilled with butter and herbs at waterfront restaurants.
A creamy comfort dish featuring fresh Malpeque oysters simmered in butter, cream, and local seasonings, often served with crusty bread.
Sweet local lobster meat tossed with mayo and served on a toasted bun, highlighting the bay's premium shellfish harvest.
A hearty soup combining oysters, mussels, lobster, and local fish in a rich, creamy broth typical of Maritime cuisine.
Fresh mussels harvested from Malpeque Bay grilled with garlic, white wine, and herbs, often served family-style at casual waterfront eateries.

culture.detail: Visit between September and April when Malpeque oysters are at their peak flavor and most restaurants feature special oyster promotions.
Plan beach walks around low tide to safely access more shoreline and avoid being cut off by the bay's significant tidal range.
Purchase directly from independent oyster farms and fishing families when possible, as shellfish harvesting is central to the bay's heritage and economy.
Maritime weather can shift rapidly; bring a windbreaker and warm layers even on sunny days, as ocean breezes keep coastal areas cool year-round.

places.name: Orwell Corner Historic Village
places.description: A preserved 19th-century rural village showcasing authentic island farm life and heritage buildings including a general store, church, and schoolhouse. Visitors can experience traditional crafts, cooking, and seasonal festivals.

activities.detail: Explore the restored 1894 general store stocked with period merchandise and learn about rural commerce and daily life in 19th-century PEI.
Visit the one-room schoolhouse to understand how island children were educated in the 1800s with period furnishings and educational tools on display.
Watch artisans demonstrate blacksmithing, weaving, and woodworking techniques authentic to the era throughout the village.
Attend a seasonal service or guided tour of the restored church building to appreciate its historical significance and architectural features.
Participate in hands-on cooking demonstrations using traditional methods and heritage recipes in the restored kitchen of the farmhouse.
Attend festivals throughout the year including Old-Time Music Festival and harvest celebrations featuring traditional music, crafts, and period activities.
Follow self-guided paths through the village grounds connecting the various heritage buildings while learning about rural island history.

food.detail: Sample traditional PEI potato recipes prepared using 19th-century cooking methods in the village kitchen, reflecting the crop's central role in island farming.
Taste freshly baked bread made in the farmhouse using heritage recipes and wood-fired ovens that demonstrate period cooking techniques.
Experience hearty meals featuring preserved and fresh root vegetables typical of what island families would have eaten year-round.
Enjoy afternoon tea with traditional shortbread and molasses cookies served in period settings that reflect island hospitality customs.

culture.detail: Consider wearing casual period-appropriate clothing to enhance your experience and photos, though modern dress is perfectly acceptable.
Visit during summer and early fall when the village is fully operational with staff, interpreters, and demonstrations available; hours are limited in off-season.
Handle artifacts and furnishings with care as they are authentic historical pieces; follow interpreter guidance when touching or photographing items.
Take time to speak with costumed staff and craftspeople who provide rich context about daily life, traditions, and the village's restoration history.

places.name: Point Prim Lighthouse
places.description: Canada's oldest and only round brick lighthouse, standing majestically on a scenic headland with sweeping views of the Northumberland Strait. The lighthouse offers visitors the chance to climb to the top for panoramic coastal views.

activities.detail: Ascend the 60 steps to the top of Canada's oldest round brick lighthouse for panoramic views of the Northumberland Strait and surrounding coastline.
Tour the restored keeper's residence adjacent to the lighthouse to learn about the history of Point Prim and lighthouse operations.
Capture stunning views of the red sand cliffs, rocky shoreline, and maritime landscape that make this headland a photographer's destination.
Stroll along the scenic walking paths surrounding the lighthouse to enjoy wild grasses, coastal vegetation, and views of the Strait.
Pack a meal and dine at the picnic tables overlooking the water, making for a memorable seaside lunch experience.
Observe migratory seabirds and local waterfowl during golden hour, when the lighthouse beacon and coastal light create ideal viewing conditions.

food.detail: Sample sweet, tender mussels harvested from local waters and prepared steamed or in cream sauce at nearby coastal restaurants.
Enjoy succulent Atlantic lobster meat tucked into a toasted bun, a classic Maritime delicacy available at seafood shacks throughout the region.
Taste briny, fresh oysters sourced from the Northumberland Strait, often served raw on the half-shell or lightly grilled.
Try sweet, buttery scallops from nearby waters, typically pan-seared and served as a restaurant staple on Prince Edward Island.
Warm up with a hearty local soup combining PEI potatoes with fresh white fish, cream, and herbs—a comfort food staple.

culture.detail: The lighthouse is typically open to visitors from May through October, so plan your visit accordingly for full access to the tower and museum.
The 1846 brick lighthouse is a protected heritage site, so handle exhibits with care and follow posted guidelines to preserve this national treasure.
Coastal weather on the Strait can change quickly, so bring layers and check conditions before climbing the tower for safety.

places.name: Victoria
places.description: A charming Victorian seaside village with colorful buildings, excellent galleries, and a celebrated dinner theatre overlooking the water. The tiny town punches well above its size with arts venues and gourmet restaurants.
imageQueries: Victoria by the Sea colorful buildings

activities.detail: Enjoy a three-course meal paired with live theatre performances in an intimate waterfront setting that has entertained audiences for decades.
Stroll through Victoria's vibrant collection of independent art galleries showcasing local and Maritime artists in converted heritage buildings.
Walk along the quiet sandy beach at the village's edge for views of the Northumberland Strait and opportunities to spot local wildlife.

activities.text: Confederation Court Mall and village shops
activities.detail: Browse eclectic boutiques, craft shops, and local artisan stores housed in charming Victorian-era buildings throughout the compact downtown.
Visit this iconic 1900 hotel building that anchors the village and represents Victoria's heritage as a thriving Victorian-era destination.
Follow the picturesque pathways around the village perimeter for photography opportunities and peaceful views of the surrounding coastline.

food.detail: Creamy Maritime chowder made with fresh PEI mussels, lobster, and local fish served in village restaurants overlooking the water.
Farm-fresh oysters from nearby beds, typically served raw on the half shell or grilled at gourmet dining establishments throughout Victoria.
Creative preparations of PEI's famous potatoes, from hand-cut fries to gourmet gratins, featured on most local restaurant menus.
A multi-course gourmet dinner specifically designed to complement evening theatre performances, combining Maritime ingredients with refined cuisine.
Seasonal treats featuring strawberries, blueberries, and raspberries grown on Island farms, showcased in pies, tarts, and ice creams.

culture.text: Book dinner theatre well in advance
culture.detail: Victoria Playhouse performances sell out quickly during summer season, so reservations should be made weeks ahead for guaranteed seating.

culture.text: Visit during summer for peak experience
culture.detail: Most galleries, restaurants, and the dinner theatre operate on seasonal schedules with June through September offering the fullest programming and warmest weather.
Victoria is intentionally car-free in most areas and designed for leisurely strolling between shops, galleries, and restaurants at a relaxed Island tempo.
Even in summer, coastal breezes and evening temperatures can be cool, so bringing layers is practical for waterfront meals and outdoor activities.

places.name: Brudenell River Provincial Park
places.description: A scenic provincial park featuring kayaking, hiking trails, and golf along a river valley with diverse ecosystems and wildlife viewing opportunities. The park offers both adventure and peaceful nature immersion on the eastern side of the island.

activities.detail: Paddle through the scenic river valley with opportunities to spot herons, eagles, and other wildlife along the tree-lined waterway.
Play an 18-hole championship course designed by Michael Hurdzan that winds through the park with river views and challenging terrain.
Explore multiple trails ranging from easy riverside walks to moderate woodland hikes through diverse ecosystems and wetlands.
Observe bald eagles, great blue herons, osprey, and white-tailed deer in their natural riverside habitat, especially during early morning hours.
Drive through the park's winding roads to experience sweeping views of the river valley and forested landscape from multiple vantage points.
Enjoy meals at designated picnic areas overlooking the Brudenell River with tables and access to nature trails.

food.detail: Sample fresh PEI mussels steamed or served in local restaurants and lodges throughout the eastern shore region.
Try a classic Maritime lobster roll with local catch served on toasted bread, available at nearby dining establishments.
Taste locally harvested oysters prepared fresh at regional restaurants within short driving distance of the park.
Enjoy PEI wild blueberry pies, tarts, and jam-based treats that celebrate the local berry harvest season.
Experience seasonal menus at nearby lodges featuring local vegetables, game, and seafood from the Brudenell River area.

culture.detail: Maintain a safe distance from animals and follow park guidelines to protect nesting birds and avoid disturbing natural habitats.
Water activities are central to the park experience; locals and visitors prioritize outdoor exploration and conservation of the river ecosystem.
Summer months (June-September) offer the best weather and wildlife activity, though the golf course operates year-round for enthusiasts.
Book accommodations and guided kayaking services through established park-affiliated operators to support the local economy.

places.name: Kensington
places.description: A quaint railway village featuring the well-preserved Kensington Train Station and charming shops and cafes along tree-lined streets. It's a picturesque stop between Charlottetown and the beaches with vintage character and local hospitality.

activities.detail: Explore the beautifully restored Victorian railway station that serves as the village's iconic landmark and cultural hub.
Walk along the tree-lined main street to discover local shops, galleries, and cafes housed in heritage buildings.
Browse antiques, collectibles, and locally-made crafts in this charming shop celebrating the village's railway heritage.
Use Kensington as a base for cycling to the nearby North Shore beaches, just 15-20 minutes away by bike.
Experience warm hospitality at family-run cafes serving traditional Maritime comfort food and homemade baked goods.

food.detail: Fresh Atlantic lobster served on a toasted roll, a PEI classic that appears on many local cafe menus.
Apple, blueberry, or strawberry pie baked fresh daily by local bakeries and cafes using island fruit.
Creamy Maritime-style chowder featuring local mussels, clams, and seasonal fish served in village restaurants.
Steamed Malpeque mussels prepared with local herbs and butter, celebrating the island's famous shellfish.

culture.detail: The village's identity centers on its railway history, so visitors should appreciate the restored station and vintage aesthetic that define Kensington.
Expect warm, friendly service and genuine conversation with shopkeepers and cafe owners who take pride in welcoming visitors personally.
Spring and fall offer the most authentic experience with fewer tourists, allowing you to connect with locals and enjoy the village's quiet charm.

places.name: Ferguson's Cove
places.description: A picturesque fishing village with authentic working wharves, seafaring heritage, and some of the island's best seafood restaurants. The sheltered cove offers an intimate look at PEI's maritime culture and traditions.

activities.detail: Walk among the active fishing docks to watch local fishermen unload their daily catch and observe traditional boat maintenance and repair.
The historic public wharf offers scenic views of the sheltered cove and is an ideal spot for photography and understanding the village's maritime heritage.
Dine at waterfront establishments where chefs prepare ultra-fresh lobster, mussels, and other daily catches prepared in traditional PEI styles.
Hike the nearby shoreline paths to experience the rugged Atlantic landscape and spot seabirds, seals, and maritime wildlife.
Engage with working fishermen at the dock during morning hours to hear authentic stories about fishing traditions, weather patterns, and island life.

food.detail: Experience whole steamed or grilled lobster caught that morning, typically served with melted butter and local vegetables at waterfront restaurants.
Locally harvested mussels steamed in white wine and garlic, showcasing the cove's abundant shellfish resources.
Hand-battered haddock or halibut fried to order and served with crispy fries, using catch from the dock just steps away.
PEI oysters sourced from nearby beds, served fresh on the half-shell with mignonette at local seafood establishments.
Plump, sweet scallops combined with local cream and herbs in a traditional Maritime preparation.

culture.detail: Visit the wharves in early morning (5–7 AM) when fishermen are actively unloading their catch for the most authentic view of working village life.
The wharves are working areas, not tourist attractions; stay clear of equipment, nets, and active dock work, and ask permission before photographing fishermen.
Fishing activity and restaurant hours vary significantly by season, with peak activity in summer and fall; winter visits may find reduced services.
Many waterfront establishments are family-run and source directly from the docks; eating at these venues directly supports the fishing community's livelihood.

places.name: Montague
places.description: A riverside town serving as the gateway to the eastern island with riverboat cruises, local shops, and restaurants celebrating island culture. The town's bridge crossing the Montague River creates an iconic picturesque scene.

activities.detail: Take a scenic cruise along the Montague River to spot local wildlife and enjoy views of the iconic bridge and surrounding marshlands.
Capture the famous red and green arched bridge spanning the Montague River, one of Prince Edward Island's most photographed landmarks.
Browse local boutiques, gift shops, and artisan stores along the main commercial strip celebrating Island crafts and products.
Visit this nearby park featuring hiking trails, picnic areas, and river access just minutes south of downtown Montague.

activities.text: Montague Regional High School Heritage Museum
activities.detail: Explore local history exhibits documenting the town's maritime heritage and development as an eastern Island gateway.
Stroll along the riverside promenades and dockside areas where fishing boats and pleasure craft create authentic maritime atmosphere.

food.detail: Enjoy sweet Atlantic lobster meat served on toasted buns at waterfront restaurants, a signature Island seafood experience.
Savor fresh PEI mussels steamed in white wine or garlic butter, sourced from nearby waters and served at casual dining spots.
Try battered and fried local white fish with hand-cut fries, often made with haddock or cod caught in the Maritime provinces.
Taste the Island's famous strawberry dessert made with locally grown berries, whipped cream, and fluffy sponge cake.
Sample local dulse snacks and seasonings derived from harvested seaweed, reflecting coastal food traditions.

culture.detail: Many local restaurants and shops have seasonal hours tied to fishing seasons and tourism patterns, so check ahead during shoulder months.
Montague operates on Island time with a relaxed rhythm; expect friendly service but unhurried transactions and outdoor activities weather-dependent.
Summer festivals, farmers markets, and community gatherings define the social calendar, and visitors are welcomed to participate in Island traditions.

places.name: New London
places.description: A tiny creative village birthplace of Lucy Maud Montgomery, now home to artist studios, galleries, and the Lucy Maud Montgomery birthplace museum. The village embodies the literary and artistic spirit that permeates PEI's cultural landscape.
imageQueries: New London Lucy Maud Montgomery birthplace

activities.detail: Tour the restored 1872 home where the beloved author of Anne of Green Gables was born, featuring period furnishings and literary memorabilia.
Visit working studios and galleries throughout the village where PEI artists create and sell paintings, sculptures, crafts, and other original works.
Explore this restored one-room schoolhouse that represents PEI's educational heritage and offers insight into 19th-century village life.
Drive a short distance to nearby Cavendish to visit the inspiration for Anne's fictional home, with period gardens, heritage buildings, and literary exhibitions.
Stroll through New London's quiet streets to admire heritage architecture, browse artist signage, and soak in the creative, literary atmosphere.

food.detail: A PEI summer staple featuring fresh local lobster meat tossed with mayo on a toasted bun, available at local eateries and food shops.
PEI strawberries layered with whipped cream and sponge cake, a seasonal dessert highlight celebrating the island's famous berries.
Battered and fried local Atlantic fish served with hand-cut fries, reflecting the island's maritime food culture.
PEI's signature ingredient appears in local restaurants as creamy potato soup, potato pancakes, and other traditional island dishes.

culture.detail: New London is sacred ground for Lucy Maud Montgomery fans; speak respectfully about the author and her connection to the village when visiting.
The village thrives on art sales and studio visits; purchasing directly from studios and galleries is deeply appreciated by the creative community.
The village is most vibrant from June through September when artist studios are fully open and the village hosts visitors; some attractions have limited winter hours.
This is a slow, quiet village meant for leisurely exploration rather than rushing; allow time to chat with artists and absorb the literary atmosphere.

restaurants.name: The Lobster on the Wharf
restaurants.description: Waterfront institution serving fresh PEI lobster and maritime cuisine with harbour views.

restaurants.name: Claddagh Oyster House
restaurants.description: Cozy Irish pub featuring PEI oysters, mussels, and creative seafood preparations.

restaurants.name: Sirenella Pasta Bar
restaurants.description: Intimate Italian restaurant known for house-made pasta and warm hospitality.

restaurants.name: Benchmark Brewery
restaurants.description: Local craft brewery with elevated pub fare and rotating seasonal beers.

restaurants.name: Terre Rouge
restaurants.description: Farm-to-table restaurant emphasizing local PEI ingredients in refined seasonal dishes.

restaurants.name: Peake's Quay
restaurants.description: Historic riverside restaurant housed in a renovated warehouse with focus on local catch.

restaurants.name: The Dunes Café
restaurants.description: Gallery-adjacent restaurant with ocean views serving creative interpretations of island ingredients.

restaurants.name: Schoolhouse Restaurant
restaurants.description: Casual country restaurant in a converted schoolhouse serving homestyle PEI cooking.

restaurants.name: The Bite House
restaurants.description: Casual sharing-focused restaurant featuring global small plates and local wines.

restaurants.name: Red Shores Brewing Company
restaurants.description: Local brewery and restaurant with house-brewed beer and hearty pub-style fare.

restaurants.name: Shipton Farm
restaurants.description: Working farm restaurant offering seasonal menus based on their own produce and local sources.

restaurants.name: Vic's on the River
restaurants.description: Riverside fine dining destination known for fresh-caught seafood and river views.

restaurants.name: Café Olé
restaurants.description: Vibrant casual spot serving authentic Mexican cuisine and creative cocktails.

restaurants.name: St. Lawrence Restaurant
restaurants.description: Upscale fine dining restaurant featuring refined Acadian-inspired cuisine with wine pairings.
